fact: Orville Wright made the first powered, sustained, and controlled airplane flight, covering 120 feet in 12 seconds at Kill Devil Hills, North Carolina
The Wright Brothers were from Dayton, Ohio, where they ran a bicycle repair and manufacturing shop before turning to aviation
The Wright Flyer, the aircraft used for the first powered flight, had a wingspan of 40 feet 4 inches and weighed 605 pounds
Only five people witnessed the Wright Brothers' first flight at Kill Devil Hills - three members of the Kill Devil Hills Life-Saving Station, a local businessman, and a teenage boy
The Wright Brothers' first flight was powered by a 12-horsepower gasoline engine they built themselves
Alberto Santos-Dumont flew around the Eiffel Tower in his airship No. 6, winning the Deutsch Prize of 100,000 francs
Santos-Dumont made the first officially witnessed heavier-than-air flight in Europe with the 14-bis, flying 60 meters at Bagatelle, Paris
Santos-Dumont flew 220 meters at a height of 6 meters with the 14-bis, setting the first official aviation record certified by the Aéro-Club de France
Charles Lindbergh completed the first solo non-stop transatlantic flight from New York to Paris in the Spirit of St. Louis
Charles Lindbergh landed at Le Bourget Field in Paris after flying 3,600 miles in 33 hours and 30 minutes
The Spirit of St. Louis was a custom-built Ryan NYP monoplane with a single Wright Whirlwind J-5C engine
Lindbergh won the $25,000 Orteig Prize for the first non-stop flight between New York and Paris
Amelia Earhart became the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean, landing in Londonderry, Northern Ireland
Amelia Earhart took off from Harbour Grace, Newfoundland for her solo transatlantic flight
Amelia Earhart was the first woman passenger to cross the Atlantic by air, flying from Newfoundland to Wales
Amelia Earhart set the women's altitude record of 14,000 feet in an autogyro
Amelia Earhart became the first person to fly solo from Honolulu, Hawaii to Oakland, California
Amelia Earhart disappeared over the Pacific Ocean during her attempt to circumnavigate the globe near Howland Island
Amelia Earhart's final radio transmission was received at 08:43 local time on July 2, 1937
Chuck Yeager became the first pilot to break the sound barrier in level flight, flying the Bell X-1 rocket plane at Mach 1.06
Chuck Yeager named his Bell X-1 aircraft 'Glamorous Glennis' after his wife
The Bell X-1 was dropped from a B-29 bomber at 20,000 feet before Yeager ignited its rocket engines
Chuck Yeager reached Mach 2.44 (1,650 mph) in the Bell X-1A, setting a new speed record
X-15 pilot Joe Walker set an altitude record of 354,200 feet (67 miles), qualifying as spaceflight
The X-15 program made 199 flights between 1959 and 1968, contributing crucial data for spacecraft design
The first X-15 powered flight was made by Scott Crossfield at Edwards Air Force Base
Glenn Curtiss won the first international air race, the Gordon Bennett Cup, at Reims, France, flying at 46.5 mph
Glenn Curtiss made the first long-distance flight between two major U.S. cities, flying 137 miles from Albany to New York City
Glenn Curtiss flew the first practical seaplane from the water in the United States at San Diego
Glenn Curtiss set an unofficial world motorcycle speed record of 136.36 mph on his V-8 motorcycle at Ormond Beach, Florida
Glenn Curtiss received U.S. Pilot's License #1 from the Aero Club of America (licenses were issued alphabetically)
Eugene Ely made the first airplane takeoff from a ship, launching from the deck of USS Birmingham
Eugene Ely made the first airplane landing on a ship, landing on USS Pennsylvania in San Francisco Bay using the first arresting gear system
The de Havilland Comet became the world's first commercial jet airliner, entering service with BOAC
The de Havilland Comet made its maiden commercial flight from London to Johannesburg via Rome, Beirut, Khartoum, Entebbe, and Livingstone
Two de Havilland Comet aircraft broke apart in flight due to metal fatigue around square windows, killing 56 people total, leading to the grounding of the entire fleet
The Boeing 707 made its first commercial flight, carrying passengers from New York to Paris for Pan American World Airways
The Boeing 747 'Jumbo Jet' made its maiden flight from Everett, Washington
Pan Am operated the first commercial Boeing 747 flight from New York JFK to London Heathrow
The Boeing 747 was the first wide-body commercial airliner, capable of carrying over 400 passengers
The Douglas DC-3 made its maiden flight at Clover Field in Santa Monica, California
The Douglas DC-3 revolutionized air transport and became the first airliner to make a profit carrying passengers alone, without airmail subsidies
American Airlines operated the first DC-3 passenger flight, flying from Chicago to New York
Over 16,000 DC-3s and military variants (C-47s) were built, more than any other transport aircraft
The Concorde supersonic airliner made its maiden flight at Toulouse, France
The Concorde entered commercial service simultaneously for British Airways and Air France, flying London-Bahrain and Paris-Rio de Janeiro
Concorde set the record for the fastest transatlantic crossing by a commercial aircraft, flying from New York to London in 2 hours, 52 minutes, and 59 seconds
Air France Flight 4590, a Concorde, crashed shortly after takeoff from Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport, killing 113 people and leading to the end of Concorde service
The last commercial Concorde flight was operated by British Airways from New York JFK to London Heathrow
Bessie Coleman became the first Black woman and first Native American woman to hold a pilot's license
Bessie Coleman earned her pilot's license from the Fédération Aéronautique Internationale in France because American flight schools refused to admit her
Bessie Coleman was killed in a plane crash during a rehearsal for an aerial show in Jacksonville, Florida, when she fell from her plane at 2,000 feet
Harriet Quimby became the first woman to obtain a U.S. pilot's license
Harriet Quimby became the first woman to fly across the English Channel, flying from Dover to Hardelot, France
Harriet Quimby was killed when she was thrown from her plane during a flying meet at Boston Harbor
Jacqueline Cochran became the first woman to break the sound barrier, flying an F-86 Sabre at Edwards Air Force Base
Jacqueline Cochran set more speed, altitude, and distance records than any pilot in aviation history, male or female
Jacqueline Cochran led the Women Airforce Service Pilots (WASP) during World War II, overseeing over 1,000 women pilots
Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly solo around the world, completing the journey in 29 days
Jerrie Mock took off from Columbus, Ohio in her single-engine Cessna 180 named 'Spirit of Columbus' to begin her around-the-world flight
The Tuskegee Airmen were the first Black military aviators in the U.S. Army Air Corps, trained at Tuskegee Army Air Field in Alabama
The 332nd Fighter Group (Tuskegee Airmen) flew over 15,000 sorties during World War II and never lost a bomber they were escorting to enemy fighters
Benjamin O. Davis Jr. became the first Black general in the United States Air Force and commanded the Tuskegee Airmen in combat
The Tuskegee Airmen shot down over 100 enemy aircraft during World War II and destroyed many more on the ground
The Hindenburg disaster killed 36 people when the German passenger airship caught fire and was destroyed while attempting to land at Lakehurst, New Jersey
Herbert Morrison's famous radio broadcast 'Oh, the humanity!' was recorded as he watched the Hindenburg burn
The Hindenburg was 804 feet long and was the largest aircraft ever built, filled with 7 million cubic feet of hydrogen
TWA Flight 800 exploded and crashed into the Atlantic Ocean off Long Island, New York, killing all 230 people aboard
American Airlines Flight 191 crashed at Chicago O'Hare Airport shortly after takeoff when an engine separated from the wing, killing 273 people in the deadliest aviation accident on U.S. soil
United Airlines Flight 232 crash-landed at Sioux City, Iowa after total loss of hydraulic controls, with 184 of 296 passengers surviving thanks to the crew's heroic efforts
ValuJet Flight 592 crashed into the Florida Everglades 10 minutes after takeoff from Miami, killing all 110 aboard when oxygen generators in the cargo hold caught fire
American Airlines Flight 11 was hijacked and crashed into the North Tower of the World Trade Center, killing all 92 aboard and approximately 1,600 people in the building
United Airlines Flight 175 was hijacked and crashed into the South Tower of the World Trade Center at 9:03 AM, killing all 65 aboard and approximately 900 people in the building
American Airlines Flight 77 was hijacked and crashed into the Pentagon, killing all 64 aboard and 125 people in the building
United Airlines Flight 93 was hijacked and crashed in a field near Shanksville, Pennsylvania after passengers attempted to retake the aircraft from the hijackers
Flight attendant Betty Ong made a crucial phone call from American Airlines Flight 11 during the hijacking, providing vital information to authorities
The attack on Pearl Harbor began when 353 Japanese aircraft in two waves attacked the U.S. Pacific Fleet, killing 2,403 Americans
The Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor sank or damaged 21 U.S. Navy ships, including 8 battleships, and destroyed 188 aircraft
The Doolittle Raid was the first air operation to strike the Japanese Home Islands during World War II, with 16 B-25 bombers launching from USS Hornet
Lieutenant Colonel Jimmy Doolittle led the audacious raid on Tokyo, boosting American morale four months after Pearl Harbor
The Enola Gay, a B-29 Superfortress, dropped the first atomic bomb 'Little Boy' on Hiroshima, Japan
The Enola Gay was piloted by Colonel Paul Tibbets, who named the aircraft after his mother
Bockscar dropped the second atomic bomb 'Fat Man' on Nagasaki, Japan, effectively ending World War II
The first Allied airborne invasion of D-Day began with British and American paratroopers landing in Normandy
Over 13,000 aircraft supported the D-Day invasion, the largest amphibious assault in history
Howard Hughes set a new transcontinental speed record, flying from Los Angeles to Newark in 7 hours, 28 minutes, and 25 seconds
Howard Hughes flew around the world in 3 days, 19 hours, and 17 minutes, setting a new circumnavigation record
Howard Hughes piloted the Hughes H-4 Hercules 'Spruce Goose' on its only flight, traveling about one mile at 70 feet altitude over Long Beach Harbor
The Hughes H-4 Hercules has the largest wingspan of any aircraft ever flown at 320 feet, and was built almost entirely of birch wood due to wartime restrictions
Wiley Post became the first pilot to fly solo around the world, completing the journey in 7 days, 18 hours, and 49 minutes in his Lockheed Vega 'Winnie Mae'
Wiley Post made the first around-the-world flight with a navigator, Harold Gatty, in 8 days, 15 hours, and 51 minutes
Wiley Post pioneered high-altitude flight, developing one of the first pressure suits to fly in the stratosphere
Wiley Post and humorist Will Rogers were killed when their aircraft crashed near Point Barrow, Alaska
O'Hare International Airport in Chicago was named after Edward 'Butch' O'Hare, the U.S. Navy's first flying ace and Medal of Honor recipient in World War II
Chicago O'Hare Airport opened in February 1944 as a manufacturing plant for Douglas C-54 Skymasters during World War II
O'Hare International Airport held the distinction as the world's busiest airport by passenger traffic from 1963 to 1998
The first scheduled jet service at O'Hare was an American Airlines Boeing 707 from New York to Chicago to San Francisco
O'Hare was the site of the world's first jet bridge in 1958, revolutionizing passenger boarding
Chicago Midway Airport was once nicknamed 'the busiest square mile in the world' before O'Hare took over
Pan American World Airways was founded, becoming the principal international air carrier of the United States
United Airlines was formed from a merger of four airlines: Boeing Air Transport, Pacific Air Transport, National Air Transport, and Varney Air Lines
American Airlines was created from the merger of several smaller carriers under the Aviation Corporation
Delta Air Lines began as a crop dusting operation called Huff Daland Dusters
Delta Air Lines made its first passenger flight from Dallas, Texas to Jackson, Mississippi
The Airline Deregulation Act was signed into law by President Jimmy Carter, removing federal control over airline routes and fares
The first U.S. Contract Air Mail service began when a pilot flew mail from Pasco, Washington to Elko, Nevada for Varney Air Lines
The Wright Brothers opened the first civilian flying school at Huffman Prairie, near Dayton, Ohio
Wilbur Wright died of typhoid fever at age 45 in Dayton, Ohio
Orville Wright lived to witness the dawn of supersonic flight and died at age 76 in Dayton, Ohio
The Montgolfier brothers made the first manned free balloon flight, ascending over Paris
Jean-François Pilâtre de Rozier and the Marquis d'Arlandes were the first humans to fly, aboard a Montgolfier hot air balloon
Jacques Charles and Nicolas-Louis Robert made the first manned hydrogen balloon flight, traveling 36 km from Paris
Jean-Pierre Blanchard and John Jeffries made the first aerial crossing of the English Channel by balloon
Henri Giffard flew the first powered airship, traveling 17 miles from Paris to Élancourt
Otto Lilienthal made over 2,000 successful glider flights before his fatal crash, pioneering controlled flight
Otto Lilienthal died the day after his glider crashed at Rhinow, Germany, reportedly saying 'Small sacrifices must be made'
Samuel Langley's Aerodrome A crashed into the Potomac River on its second attempt, just 9 days before the Wright Brothers' successful flight
Louis Blériot made the first airplane flight across the English Channel, winning the £1,000 prize offered by the Daily Mail
Louis Blériot flew from Les Baraques near Calais to Dover in 36 minutes, landing near Dover Castle
The first scheduled airline service began when a Benoist flying boat flew from St. Petersburg to Tampa, Florida, with one passenger
Tony Jannus piloted the first scheduled airline flight, a 23-minute journey across Tampa Bay for the St. Petersburg-Tampa Airboat Line
The Wright Brothers sold their first airplane to the U.S. Army Signal Corps for $30,000 after successful flight trials at Fort Myer
Lieutenant Thomas Selfridge became the first person to die in a powered airplane crash, when the Wright Flyer crashed during Army trials
Orville Wright was seriously injured but survived the same crash that killed Thomas Selfridge at Fort Myer, Virginia
Cal Rodgers completed the first transcontinental flight across the United States, taking 84 days to fly from New York to Long Beach, California
Cal Rodgers' transcontinental flight covered 4,231 miles with 70 stops, including numerous crash landings and repairs
The first in-flight movie was shown on an Aeromarine Airways flight from New York to Atlantic City
The first female flight attendant, Ellen Church, began working for Boeing Air Transport
Lockheed produced the P-38 Lightning, the first fighter aircraft to fly over 400 mph in level flight
The Messerschmitt Me 262 became the world's first operational jet-powered fighter aircraft, entering combat
The Gloster Meteor became the first British jet fighter and the only Allied jet to see combat in World War II
The North American P-51 Mustang, often considered the best propeller fighter of World War II, first flew
The Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress first flew, becoming one of the most important heavy bombers of World War II
The Boeing B-29 Superfortress, the most advanced bomber of World War II, made its first flight
The Curtiss NC-4 became the first aircraft to cross the Atlantic Ocean, flying from Newfoundland to Lisbon via the Azores
John Alcock and Arthur Whitten Brown completed the first non-stop transatlantic flight, from Newfoundland to Ireland in 16 hours 12 minutes
Alcock and Brown crash-landed in a bog at Clifden, Ireland after flying through fog, rain, and ice in their Vickers Vimy bomber
The first non-stop around-the-world flight without refueling was completed by Dick Rutan and Jeana Yeager in Voyager
Dick Rutan and Jeana Yeager took off from Edwards Air Force Base and returned 9 days, 3 minutes, and 44 seconds later after circling the globe
The Voyager aircraft landed with only 5 gallons of fuel remaining after its record-breaking circumnavigation
Steve Fossett became the first person to fly solo non-stop around the world without refueling in the GlobalFlyer
The SR-71 Blackbird set the absolute speed record for a piloted air-breathing jet aircraft at 2,193 mph (Mach 3.32)
The SR-71 Blackbird also set the altitude record for horizontal flight at 85,069 feet on the same day as its speed record
The F-117 Nighthawk, the first operational aircraft designed around stealth technology, made its maiden flight
The B-2 Spirit stealth bomber made its first public flight at Palmdale, California
The Lockheed U-2 'Dragon Lady' spy plane was shot down over the Soviet Union, creating an international incident
Gary Powers, the U-2 pilot, was captured by the Soviets and later exchanged for Soviet spy Rudolf Abel
The first helicopter flight by a practical single-rotor helicopter was made by Igor Sikorsky in the VS-300
Igor Sikorsky emigrated from Russia to the United States and revolutionized aviation with his helicopter designs
The Wright Brothers used Huffman Prairie, an 84-acre cow pasture outside Dayton, Ohio, to perfect their flying techniques after Kitty Hawk
The first night airmail flight was conducted between Chicago and Cheyenne, Wyoming
Jimmy Doolittle performed the first completely blind flight, taking off, flying, and landing using only instruments
The first transcontinental air service began with combined rail and air travel from New York to San Francisco taking 48 hours
The Lockheed Constellation entered commercial service, becoming the first pressurized airliner in widespread use
TWA Flight 2 and United Airlines Flight 718 collided over the Grand Canyon, killing all 128 people and leading to the creation of the FAA
The Federal Aviation Agency (later Administration) was established as a direct result of the Grand Canyon mid-air collision
The first Boeing 737, which would become the best-selling jet airliner in history, made its maiden flight
Over 10,000 Boeing 737s have been delivered, making it the most produced large commercial jet
The Airbus A380, the world's largest passenger aircraft, made its first commercial flight for Singapore Airlines from Singapore to Sydney
The Airbus A380 can carry up to 853 passengers in an all-economy configuration
The Boeing 787 Dreamliner, the first major airliner made primarily of composite materials, completed its first flight
The first commercial drone delivery was made by Amazon Prime Air in Cambridge, UK
The Predator drone conducted its first armed reconnaissance mission in Afghanistan
The Solar Impulse 2 completed the first solar-powered circumnavigation of the Earth, taking 16 months
Bertrand Piccard landed Solar Impulse 2 in Abu Dhabi, completing the 26,744-mile journey
The tilt-rotor V-22 Osprey, which can take off like a helicopter and fly like a plane, entered operational service with the Marine Corps
Pan Am Flight 103 was destroyed by a bomb over Lockerbie, Scotland, killing all 259 aboard and 11 on the ground
The Tenerife airport disaster killed 583 people when two Boeing 747s collided on the runway in the deadliest accident in aviation history
A KLM 747 attempted to take off while a Pan Am 747 was still on the runway in dense fog at Los Rodeos Airport, Tenerife
Japan Airlines Flight 123 crashed into a mountain after a catastrophic decompression, killing 520 in the deadliest single-aircraft accident
Four crew members survived the Japan Airlines Flight 123 crash, rescued 14 hours after the accident
Air France Flight 447 crashed into the Atlantic Ocean during a flight from Rio de Janeiro to Paris, killing all 228 aboard
The black boxes from Air France Flight 447 were not recovered until nearly two years after the crash
Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 disappeared during a flight from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing, becoming one of aviation's greatest mysteries
Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 was shot down over eastern Ukraine, killing all 298 people aboard
Germanwings Flight 9525 was deliberately crashed into the French Alps by the co-pilot, killing all 150 people aboard
The first Boeing 747SP, a shortened long-range version, completed a record-breaking westbound around-the-world flight in 54 hours
Captain Chesley 'Sully' Sullenberger safely landed US Airways Flight 1549 on the Hudson River after both engines were disabled by bird strikes
All 155 passengers and crew survived the 'Miracle on the Hudson' landing, making it one of the most successful emergency water landings
US Airways Flight 1549 was struck by a flock of Canada geese at approximately 2,800 feet, just minutes after takeoff from LaGuardia
The Wright Brothers' first powered flight was shorter than the wingspan of a Boeing 747
Boeing was founded in Seattle by William Boeing as the Pacific Aero Products Co.
Boeing's corporate headquarters moved from Seattle to Chicago
Douglas Aircraft Company was founded in Santa Monica, California by Donald Douglas
Douglas Aircraft merged with McDonnell Aircraft to form McDonnell Douglas
Boeing acquired McDonnell Douglas, creating the largest aerospace company in the world
Lockheed Martin was formed by the merger of Lockheed Corporation and Martin Marietta
The Airbus consortium was formally established by European aerospace companies
The first Airbus aircraft, the A300, made its maiden flight as the world's first twin-engine wide-body
Southwest Airlines began operations with flights between Dallas, Houston, and San Antonio using three Boeing 737s
JetBlue Airways began operations with a flight from New York JFK to Fort Lauderdale
The first regularly scheduled supersonic passenger service began with Concorde flights from London and Paris to Bahrain and Rio
The Soviet Tupolev Tu-144 was the first supersonic transport to fly, beating Concorde by two months
The Tupolev Tu-144 made its first passenger flight from Moscow to Alma-Ata
The Tu-144 was withdrawn from passenger service after only 55 scheduled flights due to safety concerns
The first scheduled passenger helicopter service began between Cardiff and Liverpool
The Sikorsky S-42 flying boat set eight world records and pioneered transoceanic flight routes for Pan Am
The Boeing 314 Clipper began transpacific passenger service for Pan Am, flying from San Francisco to Hong Kong
Pan Am's 'China Clipper' Martin M-130 made the first commercial transpacific airmail flight from San Francisco to Manila
The Spirit of St. Louis completed 150 flights on goodwill tours of the United States and Latin America after Lindbergh's transatlantic success
Charles Lindbergh turned down a $1 million Hollywood movie offer after his transatlantic flight
The kidnapping and murder of Charles Lindbergh Jr., the 20-month-old son of Charles and Anne Morrow Lindbergh, shocked the nation
The first practical radar system was demonstrated in Britain by Robert Watson-Watt, revolutionizing aviation safety and warfare
The RAF's use of radar during the Battle of Britain proved decisive in detecting incoming German aircraft
The first commercial flight recorder (black box) was installed on a British aircraft following BOAC Comet crashes
David Warren invented the flight data recorder in Australia after investigating the mysterious crashes of the de Havilland Comet
The Traffic Collision Avoidance System (TCAS) became mandatory on all commercial aircraft in the United States with more than 30 seats
Ground Proximity Warning Systems became mandatory on large commercial aircraft following the Eastern Air Lines Flight 401 crash
Eastern Air Lines Flight 401 crashed into the Florida Everglades while the crew was distracted by a landing gear indicator light
The first autopilot, called the 'Automatic Pilot', was demonstrated by Lawrence Sperry aboard a Curtiss flying boat
Lawrence Sperry won a 50,000-franc prize for demonstrating his autopilot with both hands off the controls at a Paris aviation exhibition
The first aerial refueling occurred between two DH-4B aircraft over San Diego, extending the receiving aircraft's endurance
The Question Mark, a modified Fokker C-2, set an endurance record of 150 hours 40 minutes using aerial refueling over Los Angeles
The Wright Brothers' patent war against Glenn Curtiss and other aviators lasted until 1917, when the U.S. government forced a resolution
The Wright Brothers' basic patent for a 'flying machine' was granted after extensive legal battles
The first fatal commercial airline accident occurred when a DH.18 crashed into a DH.9 over Poix-de-Picardie, France, killing seven people
The world's first jet airliner passenger, H.J. Winsbury, flew aboard the de Havilland Comet from London to Johannesburg
The Boeing 707's distinctive swept wings were derived from research captured from Nazi Germany after World War II
The prototype Boeing 707, known as the Dash 80, performed a dramatic barrel roll during a demonstration piloted by test pilot Tex Johnston
The Fokker Dr.I triplane, made famous by the Red Baron, entered service with the German Air Force
Manfred von Richthofen, the 'Red Baron', was the highest-scoring ace of World War I with 80 confirmed victories before being killed in action
Eddie Rickenbacker became America's top ace of World War I with 26 confirmed kills, flying a SPAD S.XIII
The first aircraft catapult launch from a ship was demonstrated on USS North Carolina
USS Langley became the U.S. Navy's first aircraft carrier, converted from the collier Jupiter
The first successful landing on a moving ship at sea was made on HMS Furious by Squadron Commander Edwin Dunning
Edwin Dunning died five days later attempting to repeat his historic landing when his aircraft was blown overboard
The Grumman F6F Hellcat achieved an 19:1 kill ratio against Japanese aircraft in World War II, one of the highest in aviation history
The first flight of a jet aircraft, the Heinkel He 178, took place in Germany
Frank Whittle ran his first turbojet engine in England, though the Germans beat him to the first jet flight
The British Gloster E.28/39 made the first British jet flight powered by a Whittle W.1 engine
The Bell P-59 Airacomet made the first American jet flight at Muroc Dry Lake (now Edwards AFB)
The Korean War saw the first jet-versus-jet combat when a U.S. F-80 shot down a North Korean MiG-15
The North American F-86 Sabre and Soviet MiG-15 dominated the skies over Korea, with U.S. pilots achieving a 10:1 kill ratio
The Berlin Airlift began, with American and British aircraft delivering supplies to West Berlin for 15 months
At the peak of the Berlin Airlift, an aircraft landed at Tempelhof Airport every 30 seconds
The Berlin Airlift delivered over 2.3 million tons of cargo in 278,000 flights before the Soviet blockade ended
The first transatlantic jet passenger service was operated by BOAC Comet 4 from London to New York
Pan Am beat BOAC to be the first U.S. airline to offer transatlantic jet service, starting Boeing 707 flights three weeks later
The Lockheed Electra turboprop entered commercial service, becoming the first American-built turboprop airliner
Two fatal crashes led to speed restrictions on the Lockheed Electra until the cause (whirl mode) was identified and fixed
The first flight around the world following a polar route was completed by a Pan Am Boeing 747SP
The Boeing 747-400 set a new distance record for a commercial aircraft, flying 10,000 miles non-stop from London to Sydney
Qantas operated the first non-stop commercial flight from Australia to London, covering 9,009 miles in about 17 hours on a Boeing 787
The first commercial flight of the Boeing 777, the largest twin-engine jet, was operated by United Airlines from London to Washington
The first passenger flight of an all-electric aircraft was made by Harbour Air using a retrofitted de Havilland Beaver in Vancouver
SpaceShipOne became the first privately funded spacecraft to reach space, winning the $10 million Ansari X Prize
Virgin Galactic's SpaceShipTwo reached space with paying passengers for the first time
The first fatal space tourist accident occurred when Virgin Galactic's SpaceShipTwo broke apart over the Mojave Desert during a test flight
The Rutan Voyager was built almost entirely of composite materials to save weight for its around-the-world flight
Burt Rutan, designer of the Voyager, also designed SpaceShipOne and numerous other innovative aircraft
The Gossamer Condor won the Kremer Prize as the first human-powered aircraft to complete a figure-eight course
Bryan Allen pedaled the Gossamer Albatross across the English Channel, winning the second Kremer Prize for human-powered flight
The first successful parachute jump from an airplane was made by Captain Albert Berry over St. Louis
Georgia 'Tiny' Broadwick became the first woman to parachute from an airplane
The first emergency parachute jump was made by Harold Harris when his aircraft became uncontrollable
The ejection seat was first used operationally by a German pilot escaping a burning Heinkel He 219
Colonel Joseph Kittinger made the highest parachute jump at the time, falling from 102,800 feet in the Excelsior project
Felix Baumgartner broke Kittinger's record by jumping from 128,100 feet, breaking the sound barrier during freefall
Alan Eustace set the current highest altitude jump record at 135,890 feet, surpassing Baumgartner
The Wright Brothers flew their first public demonstration at Huffman Prairie for an audience that included Octave Chanute
Octave Chanute, the 'Father of Aviation' who mentored the Wright Brothers, published 'Progress in Flying Machines' which surveyed all prior attempts at flight
The Smithsonian Institution initially refused to recognize the Wright Brothers' achievement, falsely claiming Samuel Langley flew first
The Wright Flyer was displayed at the Science Museum in London from 1928 to 1948 because of the Smithsonian controversy
The Wright Flyer was finally installed at the Smithsonian Institution after the museum acknowledged the Wrights' achievement
A piece of fabric from the original Wright Flyer was carried to the moon aboard Apollo 11
A piece of the Wright Flyer's propeller and wing fabric flew on Mars aboard the Ingenuity helicopter
The Ingenuity helicopter made the first powered controlled flight on another planet, flying for 39.1 seconds on Mars
The Wright Brothers' first airplane engine developed only 12 horsepower but weighed just 180 pounds, an excellent power-to-weight ratio for its time
The Wright Brothers tested over 200 wing designs in a wind tunnel they built in their Dayton bicycle shop
Alberto Santos-Dumont committed suicide in 1932, reportedly distraught over the use of aircraft as weapons of war in the Brazilian Civil War
Santos-Dumont designed the Demoiselle, considered the first series-produced aircraft, offering the plans for free to encourage aviation
Santos-Dumont's friend Louis Cartier designed the first practical wristwatch for him so he could check time while flying
Boeing Air Transport introduced the first flight attendants, hiring registered nurses to care for passengers and calm their fears
TWA Flight 2 and United Airlines Flight 718 collided over the Grand Canyon because air traffic control coverage did not extend over the sparsely populated area
The collision over the Grand Canyon led Congress to establish the Federal Aviation Agency to manage civil aviation and airspace
Boeing rolled out the first 747 on September 30, 1968, with 26 airlines having already ordered the revolutionary aircraft
The Boeing 747's upper deck was originally designed as a cargo hold, giving the aircraft its distinctive hump
The first pilot to fly a Boeing 747 in commercial service was Captain Virgil Vaughn for Pan Am
EgyptAir Flight 990 crashed into the Atlantic Ocean after a co-pilot deliberately put the aircraft into a dive, killing all 217 aboard
SilkAir Flight 185 crashed into a river in Indonesia after the captain deliberately crashed the plane, killing all 104 aboard
The first woman airline pilot in the United States was Helen Richey, hired by Central Airlines
Emily Howell Warner became the first woman to be hired as a pilot by a major U.S. commercial airline (Frontier Airlines)
Beverley Bass became the first female captain of a major U.S. airline (American Airlines)
The Tuskegee Airmen's 332nd Fighter Group adopted distinctive red tail markings, earning them the nickname 'Red Tails'
The 99th Pursuit Squadron, the first Tuskegee Airmen unit, flew its first combat mission over the Mediterranean
Charles McGee of the Tuskegee Airmen flew 409 combat missions across World War II, Korea, and Vietnam - one of the highest totals in Air Force history
The Tuskegee Airmen were awarded the Congressional Gold Medal collectively for their service
President Harry Truman signed Executive Order 9981, desegregating the U.S. Armed Forces, influenced partly by the Tuskegee Airmen's success
The first African American to earn a pilot's license in the United States was Emory Malick
Willa Brown became the first African American woman to earn a commercial pilot's license in the United States
The first African American airline pilot for a major U.S. carrier was David Harris, hired by American Airlines
Patrice Clarke Washington became the first Black female captain for a major U.S. airline (United Parcel Service)
The deadliest year in commercial aviation history was 1972, with 2,429 fatalities in multiple accidents
The year 2017 was the safest year in commercial aviation history with zero fatal accidents on scheduled passenger jets
Aloha Airlines Flight 243 landed safely despite losing a large section of its fuselage in flight, demonstrating improved aircraft design
Flight attendant Clarabelle Lansing was the only fatality when she was swept out of Aloha Airlines Flight 243 during the explosive decompression
British Airways Flight 5390 landed safely after the captain was partially sucked out of a blown-out windscreen, held by crew until landing
DHL Flight 611 safely landed after being struck by a surface-to-air missile over Baghdad, the first successful landing of a civilian aircraft hit by a missile
Qantas Flight 32 safely landed with over 650 holes in its wings and fuselage after an engine exploded, in one of the most remarkable saves in aviation history
American Airlines retired its fleet of McDonnell Douglas MD-80s after 36 years of service, marking the end of an era
The Boeing 727, which dominated short-haul aviation for decades, made its last commercial passenger flight
The final Boeing 747 was delivered to Atlas Air, ending production of the 'Queen of the Skies' after 54 years
Airbus delivered its 10,000th aircraft, an A350-900 to Singapore Airlines
Boeing delivered its 10,000th 737 to Southwest Airlines
The Embraer E190 became the first commercial airliner designed with fly-by-wire technology outside Boeing and Airbus
The first commercial aircraft to have winglets as standard equipment was the Boeing 747-400
NASA's X-59 Quiet SuperSonic Technology aircraft was designed to reduce sonic booms to a quiet thump, potentially reviving supersonic commercial flight
The last flight of the SR-71 Blackbird set speed records from Los Angeles to Washington D.C. in 1 hour, 4 minutes, and 20 seconds
The Reno Air Races, featuring aircraft flying at over 500 mph just feet above the ground, began at Reno Stead Airport
The National Air Races were discontinued after a modified P-51 Mustang crashed into spectators at the Reno Air Races, killing 11 people
The Ford Trimotor 'Tin Goose', one of the first all-metal commercial aircraft, made its first flight
Admiral Richard Byrd claimed to have made the first flight over the North Pole, though the claim remains disputed
Roald Amundsen, Lincoln Ellsworth, and Umberto Nobile made the first verified flight over the North Pole in the airship Norge
Richard Byrd made the first flight over the South Pole in a Ford Trimotor
The first nonstop flight across the Pacific Ocean from Japan to the United States was completed by Clyde Pangborn and Hugh Herndon
Pangborn and Herndon jettisoned their landing gear over Japan to reduce weight and later belly-landed in Washington state after 41 hours in the air
The first regular transatlantic passenger service began between New York and Marseilles using flying boats
BOAC and Qantas began the first regular passenger service between Britain and Australia using flying boats
The last scheduled seaplane airline service in the United States ended when Chalk's International Airlines ceased operations
Leonardo da Vinci sketched designs for flying machines including an ornithopter and aerial screw around 1485
George Cayley built and flew the first successful human-carrying glider, piloted by his coachman
The coachman reportedly quit after landing Cayley's glider, saying 'I was hired to drive, not fly'
Percy Pilcher died in a glider crash, possibly just weeks away from attempting powered flight before the Wright Brothers
Gustave Whitehead claimed to have made a powered flight in Connecticut two years before the Wright Brothers, though this claim is disputed
The Fédération Aéronautique Internationale officially recognized the Wright Brothers as the first to achieve powered, controlled flight
The first aircraft radio communication was demonstrated by Frederick Baldwin and John McCurdy flying Alexander Graham Bell's Silver Dart
The first successful air mail delivery was made by Fred Wiseman, flying from Petaluma to Santa Rosa, California
The first official U.S. air mail service was established between Washington D.C. and New York, with a stop in Philadelphia
The first pilot of the U.S. air mail service flew the wrong direction on his inaugural flight, landing in Maryland instead of Philadelphia
Night air mail service required construction of beacon towers every 10 miles to guide pilots across the country
Charles Lindbergh flew the mail route between St. Louis and Chicago before his famous transatlantic flight
The first hostile act of aerial warfare occurred when Italian pilot Giulio Gavotti dropped grenades on Turkish troops in Libya
The first aerial dogfight occurred during World War I when pilots began shooting at each other with pistols and rifles
Roland Garros became the first pilot to shoot down an enemy aircraft using a forward-firing machine gun with metal deflector plates on the propeller
Anthony Fokker developed the interrupter gear allowing machine guns to fire through spinning propellers without hitting the blades
The Fokker Eindecker monoplane with synchronized machine gun caused the 'Fokker Scourge' period of German air superiority
Boeing delivered the first production B-52 Stratofortress to the U.S. Air Force
The B-52 Stratofortress has been in continuous service for over 70 years and is expected to remain operational until the 2050s
The first landing of an aircraft on a ship underway was made by Edwin Dunning on HMS Furious
The first purpose-built aircraft carrier, HMS Argus, entered service with a full-length flight deck
The U.S. Navy's first aircraft, the Curtiss A-1 Triad, was delivered to the Navy
The Navy's first pilot, Lieutenant Theodore G. Ellyson, received pilot certificate No. 28 from the Aero Club of America
The Curtiss JN-4 'Jenny' became the standard trainer for World War I pilots, with over 6,800 produced
Surplus Jennys after World War I created the barnstorming era, with pilots performing stunts and offering rides across America
Charles Lindbergh flew as a barnstormer and parachutist before becoming a mail pilot and eventually crossing the Atlantic
The Wright Brothers made four flights on December 17, 1903, with the fourth and final flight covering 852 feet
Lincoln Beachey became America's most famous stunt pilot, known as the 'Master Birdman' before his death in 1915
Lincoln Beachey died when his aircraft broke apart during a dive at the Panama-Pacific Exposition in San Francisco
Cal Rodgers drank grape soda during his transcontinental flight, leading to a sponsorship by Vin Fiz grape soda
The Vin Fiz Flyer required nearly 70 repairs and replacements during its 84-day transcontinental journey
Air France was created by the merger of five French airlines
British European Airways and British Overseas Airways Corporation merged to form British Airways
Lufthansa resumed commercial operations after being re-founded following World War II
Japan Airlines was founded and began domestic operations
Singapore Airlines was established after splitting from Malaysia-Singapore Airlines
Emirates airline was founded in Dubai with backing from the Dubai royal family
Ryanair, Europe's largest low-cost carrier, was founded in Ireland
EasyJet, a British low-cost carrier, made its first flight from London Luton to Glasgow
Spirit Airlines pioneered the ultra-low-cost carrier model in the United States
The first Around-the-World air route was established by Pan Am
The first polar route commercial flight was operated by Scandinavian Airlines from Copenhagen to Los Angeles
The Gimli Glider incident occurred when an Air Canada Boeing 767 ran out of fuel and glided to a safe landing
The Gimli Glider ran out of fuel due to a confusion between metric and imperial fuel calculations
Captain Robert Pearson glided the Boeing 767 to an emergency landing on a former RCAF runway in Gimli, Manitoba
The first use of GPS navigation on a commercial aircraft was demonstrated by Continental Airlines
The Boeing 757 was certified for Extended Twin Operations (ETOPS) allowing twin-engine aircraft on long overwater routes
The first commercial ETOPS flight was operated by TWA from Boston to Paris using a Boeing 767
Singapore Airlines launched the world's longest non-stop flight from Singapore to Newark using an A340-500
The longest non-stop commercial flight as of 2024 is Singapore Airlines' Singapore to New York JFK route at 9,527 miles
Qantas has never had a fatal accident involving a jet aircraft in its history
The last fatal accident involving a major U.S. airline was Colgan Air Flight 3407 near Buffalo, New York
Colgan Air Flight 3407 crashed killing 50 people, leading to major changes in pilot training requirements
The first successful auto-land system was demonstrated on a BEA Trident at London Heathrow
The first fully automatic landing in commercial service was performed by a BEA Trident in fog at Heathrow
The first aircraft to exceed 1,000 mph was the Bell X-1A piloted by Chuck Yeager
The McDonnell Douglas F-15 Eagle achieved a 104-0 kill ratio in air-to-air combat, never being shot down by an enemy aircraft
The F-22 Raptor became the world's first operational fifth-generation stealth fighter
The F-35 Lightning II made its first flight at Fort Worth, Texas
The A-10 Thunderbolt II 'Warthog' was designed specifically for close air support with its 30mm GAU-8 cannon
The AC-130 gunship first entered combat during the Vietnam War
The C-130 Hercules has been in continuous production longer than any other military aircraft
The first aerial combat victory using a guided missile was scored by a Taiwanese F-86 using an AIM-9 Sidewinder against a Chinese MiG-17
The USAF Thunderbirds demonstration team was established
The Blue Angels, the U.S. Navy's flight demonstration squadron, was established
The Red Arrows, the Royal Air Force aerobatic team, performed their first public display
The Snowbirds, Canada's military aerobatic team, was formed
The first in-flight refueling boom system was developed by Boeing for the KC-97 tanker
The KC-135 Stratotanker entered service, becoming the backbone of U.S. aerial refueling capability
AWACS (Airborne Warning and Control System) entered service with the U.S. Air Force aboard the E-3 Sentry
The first flight of a tiltrotor aircraft was made by the Bell XV-3
The AH-64 Apache attack helicopter entered service with the U.S. Army
The UH-60 Black Hawk helicopter entered service, replacing the UH-1 Huey
The UH-1 Huey became iconic during the Vietnam War, with over 7,000 deployed in the conflict
The CH-47 Chinook heavy-lift helicopter made its first flight
The MV-22 Osprey achieved initial operational capability with the Marine Corps after decades of development
The RQ-4 Global Hawk became the first unmanned aircraft to cross the Pacific Ocean
The MQ-1 Predator drone first flew over Bosnia during peacekeeping operations
The Wright Military Flyer became the world's first military aircraft after acceptance by the U.S. Army
The SPAD S.XIII became the most-produced French fighter of World War I with over 8,400 built
The Sopwith Camel, famous for shooting down more enemy aircraft than any other Allied fighter in WWI, first flew
The Supermarine Spitfire, Britain's iconic World War II fighter, made its first flight
The Hawker Hurricane actually shot down more aircraft during the Battle of Britain than the Spitfire
The Mitsubishi A6M Zero dominated the Pacific at the start of WWII with its exceptional range and maneuverability
The Republic P-47 Thunderbolt became the largest and heaviest single-engine piston fighter ever built
The Vought F4U Corsair achieved an 11:1 kill ratio against Japanese aircraft in World War II
The Lockheed P-80 Shooting Star became the first operational jet fighter in U.S. service
The Mikoyan-Gurevich MiG-21 became the most-produced supersonic aircraft in aviation history with over 11,000 built
The General Dynamics F-16 Fighting Falcon became one of the most successful fighter programs with over 4,600 produced
The first supersonic bomber, the Convair B-58 Hustler, entered service
The North American XB-70 Valkyrie reached Mach 3, the fastest bomber ever built, but the program was cancelled
The Rockwell B-1 Lancer supersonic bomber entered service after years of political controversy
The Northrop Grumman B-2 Spirit stealth bomber costs approximately $2.1 billion per aircraft, the most expensive aircraft ever built
The de Havilland Mosquito, built largely of wood, became one of the fastest and most versatile aircraft of World War II
The Douglas SBD Dauntless sank more Japanese shipping than any other aircraft during World War II
The Grumman TBF Avenger torpedo bomber, flown by future President George H.W. Bush, entered service
American pilot Joseph P. Kennedy Jr., older brother of JFK, was killed when his explosives-laden aircraft detonated prematurely over England
The Wright Cyclone R-1820 engine powered many famous aircraft including the B-17 Flying Fortress
The Pratt & Whitney R-2800 Double Wasp was the most powerful radial engine of World War II
The Rolls-Royce Merlin engine powered the Spitfire, Hurricane, Lancaster, and P-51 Mustang
The General Electric CF6 engine became one of the most successful large turbofans, powering Boeing 747s and 767s
The Pratt & Whitney JT9D was the first high-bypass turbofan engine, developed for the Boeing 747
The Rolls-Royce Trent engine family has powered Airbus and Boeing wide-body aircraft for over 30 years
The CFM International CFM56 became the best-selling jet engine in history with over 30,000 delivered
The General Electric GE90 holds the record as the most powerful jet engine ever, producing over 115,000 pounds of thrust
Pan American World Airways ceased operations after 64 years, ending an era in American aviation
TWA (Trans World Airlines) was acquired by American Airlines, ending another legendary airline
Eastern Air Lines, once one of the 'Big Four' U.S. airlines, ceased operations
Braniff International Airways, known for colorful aircraft liveries, ceased operations
Continental Airlines merged with United Airlines, creating one of the world's largest airlines
US Airways merged with American Airlines, completing the consolidation of major U.S. carriers
Northwest Airlines merged with Delta Air Lines in the largest airline merger at that time
Southwest Airlines became the world's largest low-cost carrier by passengers carried
The TSA (Transportation Security Administration) was created in response to the September 11 attacks
The first sky marshals (armed law enforcement on aircraft) were deployed by the U.S. in response to hijackings
The D.B. Cooper hijacking occurred when a man parachuted from a Boeing 727 with $200,000 in ransom and was never found
The Air France Flight 8969 hijacking was ended by French special forces storming the aircraft in Marseille
The Entebbe raid rescued 102 hostages from a hijacked Air France aircraft at Entebbe Airport, Uganda
Ethiopian Airlines Flight 961 crashed in the Indian Ocean after hijackers refused to allow refueling, killing 125 of 175 aboard
The first reinforced cockpit doors became mandatory on commercial aircraft following the 9/11 attacks
The 'sterile cockpit' rule, prohibiting non-essential activities below 10,000 feet, was implemented after Eastern Air Lines Flight 212 crash
The first Crew Resource Management (CRM) training was developed after the United Airlines Flight 173 fuel exhaustion accident
The Gulfstream G650 became the fastest civilian aircraft in production, capable of Mach 0.925
The Cessna 172 Skyhawk became the most produced aircraft in history with over 44,000 built
The Piper J-3 Cub trained more pilots than any other aircraft during World War II
The Beechcraft Bonanza has been in continuous production since 1947, the longest of any aircraft
The Learjet 23, the first mass-produced business jet, entered service
Bill Lear founded Learjet and revolutionized business aviation with small, fast executive jets
The Cirrus SR22 became the best-selling single-engine piston aircraft in the world
The Cirrus Airframe Parachute System (CAPS) has saved over 200 lives through whole-aircraft parachute deployments
The Robinson R22 became the best-selling light helicopter, making rotorcraft accessible to many pilots
The Bell 206 JetRanger became one of the most successful commercial helicopters with over 7,000 built
Sikorsky delivered the first production S-76 executive helicopter
Air ambulance service using helicopters was pioneered during the Korean War, saving thousands of wounded soldiers
The first commercial helicopter airline service was established by Los Angeles Airways using Sikorsky S-51s
Chicago Helicopter Airways became one of the first successful scheduled helicopter airlines in the United States
The AN-225 Mriya, the largest aircraft ever built, made its first flight in Kiev, Ukraine
The AN-225 Mriya was destroyed during the Russian invasion of Ukraine at Hostomel Airport
The Antonov An-124 Ruslan is the world's largest production aircraft still in service
The Hughes H-1 Racer set the world landplane speed record of 352 mph, flown by Howard Hughes
The Supermarine S.6B set the world speed record of 407 mph and won the Schneider Trophy permanently for Britain
The Schneider Trophy races drove rapid advances in aircraft design and directly led to the Spitfire fighter
The first aircraft exceeding 600 mph in level flight was the Gloster Meteor F.4
The Douglas D-558-II Skyrocket became the first aircraft to exceed Mach 2
